_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Modules practice 1
questions.prompt: Which syntax imports a named export called `add`?
choices: import { add } from "./math.js";
import add with "./math.js";
include add from "./math.js";
require { add } "./math.js";
questions.explanation: Named imports use braces around the exact exported name and a from clause containing the module path.

questions.title: Modules practice 2
questions.prompt: How often is a module normally evaluated when imported by several files?
choices: Once, then its exports are reused
Once per function call
Twice for named exports
It is never evaluated
questions.explanation: The module system evaluates one module instance and shares its exported bindings with the files that import it.

questions.title: Trace Modules
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The exporting file defines one reusable operation, and the importing file names the same exported binding. The resulting output is '12'.

questions.title: Modules good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules?
choices: Give each module one clear responsibility.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Give each module one clear responsibility. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: import { double } from "./math.js";
import double from "./math.js";
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A named export uses braces when imported. A default export uses the brace-free form, so the two styles must agree.

questions.title: Modules practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Paths are part of the contract'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

A correct export can still fail to load when its path is wrong. Relative browser imports normally include the file extension, and a server must deliver the module with an appropriate JavaScript content type.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: A correct export can still fail to load when its path is wrong. Relative browser imports normally include the file extension, and a server must deliver the module with an appropriate JavaScript content type.

questions.title: Modules practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Export only values other files need.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Export only values other files need. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Match named and default import styles.
questions.explanation: Match named and default import styles. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Modules?
choices: Check relative paths and file extensions.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Check relative paths and file extensions. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Modules practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The exporting file defines one reusable operation, and the importing file names the same exported binding. The output is '12'.
